front: What is Measurement in Capacity Planning?
back: The process of quantifying performance, utilization, and behavior of IT infrastructure components to establish baselines, identify bottlenecks, and predict future resource requirements.
The number of work units completed by a system per unit of time (e.g., transactions per second, requests per minute).
The total time taken to complete a single request from the moment it is submitted until the response is received by the user.
The delay between a request being made and the first byte of response being received; often measured in milliseconds.
The percentage of time a resource is busy performing useful work versus total available time; typically expressed as a percentage.
A set of benchmark measurements representing normal system behavior under typical workload conditions, used for comparison during capacity planning.
The process of describing a workload in terms of its resource demands, patterns, and profiles to understand its impact on system performance.
The process of identifying the component or resource that limits overall system performance due to high utilization or contention.
A contract defining expected performance metrics (response time, availability, throughput) that the IT service must meet.
The standardized process of running predefined tests to compare system performance against known standards or other systems.
The time required to increase or decrease capacity when workload changes; crucial for planning scalable systems.
Quantifiable metrics used to evaluate success in meeting operational and strategic objectives; essential for capacity planning decisions.
The workload level at which performance degrades significantly because resources are fully utilized; beyond this, response times increase exponentially.
The actual time a resource spends processing a request, excluding waiting time in queues.
The time a request spends waiting in a queue before being processed by a resource.
